1.pericope_label: Sing to the Lord a New Song
1.synthesis: “Oh sing to the LORD a new song… Declare his glory among the nations” (vv.1–3). The psalm summons all the earth to worship, for “the LORD made the heavens” while “all the gods of the peoples are worthless idols” (v.5); it calls the nations to ascribe glory and bring offerings, and even the heavens, sea, and fields to rejoice, “for he comes to judge the earth… in righteousness” (vv.11–13). The integrative thread is missionary praise summoning all creation and all nations to worship the coming Judge-King: the new song bursts the bounds of Israel, calling the peoples to forsake idols and the whole creation to rejoice that the LORD is coming to set the world right.
1.historical_context: Psalm 96 (largely paralleled in 1 Chronicles 16:23–33, sung at the ark’s installation) is an enthronement and missionary psalm calling “all the earth” and “the nations” (v.1, v.3) to worship. Its polemic against idols as “worthless” (v.5) and its summons to bring offerings into his courts (v.8) anticipate the ingathering of the nations. The coming of the LORD “to judge the earth” (v.13) is cause for universal rejoicing.
1.literary_structure: The “new song” (v.1) frames a psalm of expanding scope — from Israel to the nations (v.3) to the cosmos (v.11) — climaxing in the coming Judge (v.13). The repeated “ascribe to the LORD” (vv.7–8) and the rejoicing creation give the psalm its sweeping, doxological architecture.
1.theological_application: God’s glory is to be declared “among the nations” (v.3); worship is inherently missionary, summoning all peoples from worthless idols to the living God (v.5; Matthew 28:19). The “new song” befits the new mercies and the new covenant (Lamentations 3:23). The believer is moved to praise that reaches beyond himself to the nations and to long for the day when the coming Judge sets all things right, joining even creation’s rejoicing (Romans 8:19–21). God’s glory is to be declared “among the nations” (v.3); worship is inherently missionary, summoning all peoples from worthless idols to the living God (v.5; Matthew 28:19). The “new song” befits new mercies and the new covenant (Lamentations 3:23). The believer is moved to praise that reaches beyond himself to the nations and to long for the day when the coming Judge sets all things right, a longing creation itself shares (Romans 8:19–21).
1.christology: The LORD who “comes to judge the earth in righteousness” (v.13) comes in Christ, appointed to judge the world (Acts 17:31), whose coming is the world’s hope, not dread, for those who are his. The summons to declare his glory among the nations is fulfilled in the gospel to all peoples (Matthew 28:19), and the “new song” is sung at last by the redeemed from every tribe before the Lamb (Revelation 5:9). In Christ the nations forsake idols and worship the true God (1 Thessalonians 1:9).
